title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing the impact of social media on journalism.
extracts.audio_script: Man: It’s incredible how quickly a story breaks now, isn't it? Minutes after an event, it’s all over the internet. Woman: It is, but [1]I’m increasingly wary of the lack of editorial oversight in these 'citizen journalist' posts[/1]. Accuracy often takes a backseat to speed. Man: True, but traditional outlets can be just as biased, often sitting on a story to fit a political narrative. At least with social media, you get the raw data. Woman: But [2]is it really 'data' if it's just a ten-second clip without any context?[/2] It’s so easy to misinterpret what’s actually happening. Man: I agree context is key. But you can't deny that social media has democratised the news. We aren't just passive consumers anymore. Woman: Democratised, perhaps. But it’s also created a minefield of misinformation that’s becoming nearly impossible to navigate.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It’s incredible how quickly a story breaks now, isn't it? Minutes after an event, it’s all over the internet.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It is, but I’m increasingly wary of the lack of editorial oversight in these 'citizen journalist' posts. Accuracy often takes a backseat to speed.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: True, but traditional outlets can be just as biased, often sitting on a story to fit a political narrative. At least with social media, you get the raw data.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: But is it really 'data' if it's just a ten-second clip without any context? It’s so easy to misinterpret what’s actually happening.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree context is key. But you can't deny that social media has democratised the news. We aren't just passive consumers anymore.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Democratised, perhaps. But it’s also created a minefield of misinformation that’s becoming nearly impossible to navigate.

questions.question: The woman is concerned that modern news reporting...

options.A: is becoming too expensive to produce.
options.B: prioritises being first over being correct.
options.C: is controlled by too few political interests.

questions.explanation: She states 'accuracy often takes a backseat to speed', meaning speed is more important than being right.

questions.question: Why does the woman question the 'raw data' mentioned by the man?

options.A: It is often deliberately falsified by experts.
options.B: It is too difficult for the average person to understand.
options.C: It can be misleading without a broader explanation.

questions.explanation: She asks if clips without context can be considered data, noting they are 'easy to misinterpret'.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, a man and a woman, talking about a 'zero-waste' lifestyle.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: I’ve finally managed to go a whole month without buying anything in plastic packaging! Man: That’s impressive, Libby. I tried it for a week and [3]the sheer amount of pre-planning required was exhausting[/3]. I felt like I was spending my whole life in specialty shops. Woman: It does take a bit of getting used to, but you eventually find a rhythm. And it’s so much more satisfying than just tossing things in the bin. Man: I don't doubt it. But [4]don't you think the premium you pay for those bulk-buy stores is a bit prohibitive?[/4] Not everyone can afford to save the planet. Woman: Actually, I’ve found that because I’m not buying processed convenience food, my overall grocery bill has actually gone down. Man: Really? That’s surprising. I always assumed it would be the other way around. Woman: You just have to buy in season and be prepared to cook from scratch. It’s a lifestyle shift, for sure.

dialogue.text: I’ve finally managed to go a whole month without buying anything in plastic packaging!

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: That’s impressive, Libby. I tried it for a week and the sheer amount of pre-planning required was exhausting. I felt like I was spending my whole life in specialty shops.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It does take a bit of getting used to, but you eventually find a rhythm. And it’s so much more satisfying than just tossing things in the bin.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I don't doubt it. But don't you think the premium you pay for those bulk-buy stores is a bit prohibitive? Not everyone can afford to save the planet.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Actually, I’ve found that because I’m not buying processed convenience food, my overall grocery bill has actually gone down.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Really? That’s surprising. I always assumed it would be the other way around.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: You just have to buy in season and be prepared to cook from scratch. It’s a lifestyle shift, for sure.

questions.question: What did the man find most difficult about his attempt at a zero-waste lifestyle?

options.A: The lack of variety in the products available.
options.B: The time needed to organise his shopping.
options.C: The social pressure from his peers.

questions.explanation: He says the 'sheer amount of pre-planning required was exhausting', which refers to the time and effort of organization.

questions.question: What is the man's assumption about 'zero-waste' shopping?

options.A: It is only suitable for people living in large cities.
options.B: It results in a lower quality of food.
options.C: It is a more expensive way to live.

questions.explanation: He mentions the 'premium' (extra cost) and says 'not everyone can afford' it, suggesting he thinks it's expensive.

extracts.context: You hear two students, a man and a woman, discussing their upcoming graduation.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Can you believe we’re actually finishing next month? It feels like we only started last week. Woman: I know! I’m [5]terrified of the prospect of entering the job market right now[/5], though. Everything seems so precarious. Man: Tell me about it. But at least we've got our degrees. That has to count for something, right? Woman: I hope so. But sometimes I feel like [6]the skills we've learned are already becoming obsolete with all this new tech[/6]. Man: You’re being too hard on yourself. The critical thinking and research skills are universal. They’ll never go out of style. Woman: I suppose. I’m just worried I’ll end up in a role that has nothing to do with what I studied. Man: Most people do! It’s about the journey, Bella. We’ve grown so much as people, and that’s what really matters. Woman: Spoken like someone who already has an internship lined up!

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I know! I’m terrified of the prospect of entering the job market right now, though. Everything seems so precarious.

questions.question: How does the woman feel about the immediate future?

options.B: Indifferent to the outcome of her applications.

questions.explanation: She says she is 'terrified' because everything seems 'precarious' (unstable/dangerous), indicating anxiety about the economy.

questions.question: What specific concern does the woman have about her university education?

options.A: The curriculum was too focused on theory.
options.B: The knowledge she gained is rapidly dating.
options.C: The university didn't provide enough career support.

questions.explanation: She says her skills are 'becoming obsolete' (no longer useful/dated) due to new technology.
